Best food options:
	•	Boiled boneless chicken (skinless, no oil/spices)
	•	Plain white rice or boiled potato (mashed)
	•	Boiled pumpkin (helps with digestion)
	•	Rice water or chicken broth (clear, no seasoning, helps hydration)

-Fatty foods (can trigger pancreatitis)
-Milk or dairy (often worsen cramping/diarrhea)
- Bones, raw meat, spicy or salty food

Report Advice:
Basic Feeding Guidelines (3-Month-Old Kitten)
Age: 12 weeks
Frequency: 4–5 small meals per day
Water: Always keep fresh, clean water available
MORNING
Option 1:
2–3 tablespoons of high-quality kitten dry kibble soaked in warm water or kitten milk for 10–15 min.
Option 2:
Boiled egg yolk (½ yolk) + 1 teaspoon plain boiled chicken (shredded)

MID MORNING
Kitten milk replacer about 30–40 ml
OR small amount of plain curd (1 tsp) occasionally (if tolerated, not daily)
LUNCH
Home-cooked option:
Boiled chicken or fish (boneless) : 2–3 tablespoons
Small spoon of cooked rice or pumpkin for fiber
Few drops of sunflower oil for omega-6


EVENING
2–3 tablespoons of kitten kibble (dry or soaked)
You can mix a bit of chicken broth or kitten gravy for taste

NIGHT
Kitten milk replacer (20–30 ml)
Or a light meal of wet food

AVOID
Cow/buffalo milk (causes diarrhea)
Onion, garlic, chocolate, bones, or spicy food

Report Reason: Puppy presented with abnormal head posture ( head bending and intermittent backward movement for the past few days)
Report History: Accident
Report DiagnosticImaging: No distinct structural changes were visible on this view but there is a doubt of slight bending at the level of the atlas (C1 vertebra).
Report Advice:
-Recommend taking 2–3 additional X-ray views (lateral, ventro-dorsal, and oblique cervical spine views) to clearly evaluate the atlas and adjacent cervical vertebrae.
-Multiple views are necessary to confirm or rule out subtle atlas deviation, early cervical injury, congenital anomaly, or muscle spasm–related positional change.
-Until diagnosis is confirmed, advise strict rest, avoid rough play, and monitor for worsening signs (pain, vocalization, inability to hold head up).
-If repeat X-rays remain inconclusive, consider neurological evaluation.

Report Note:
Dead ||
Uterine neoplasia with secondary Pyometra caused endotoxin mediated endothelial dysfunction, sepsis induced hypercoagulability and venous stasis, fulfilling virchows triad and resulting in systemic thrombosis, splenic infarction and post operative unilateral hindlimb oedema

Detailed explanation of death
The uterine tumor predisposed the dog to secondary Pyometra resulting in chronic bacterial infection and endotoxemia. Circulating bacterial endotoxins triggered a severe systemic inflammatory response causing endothelial injury, activation of coagulation cascade and development of a marked hypercoagulable state. These changes fulfilled birchows triad leading to systemic thrombosis. Evidence of thromboembolic disease was suggested intraoperstively by the presence of a pale white margin on the spleen consistent with splenic ischemia or infarction. Postoperativelu, the development of unilateral oedema indicated venous thrombosis involving the pelvic or femoral veins.
Progressive septicemia, compounded by surgery related stress resulted in worsening circulatory dysfunction, microvascular thrombosis, impaired tissue perfusion and probable disseminated intravascular coagulation. These events culminated in multiple organ dysfunction and cardiovascular collapse leading to death.

||Final cause of death ||
Septic shock with thromboembolic complications secondary to uterine neoplasia and Pyometra.

Report Reason: Home visit IV fluid therapy due to diagnosed renal insufficiency with reduced appetite and lethargy
Report History: Patient has been showing decreased appetite, lethargy and reduced water intake for the past few days.Saline was done on 13,14,16,17,18 (today) December 2025.

18 Dec 2025
Aluminum hydroxide tablets 500 mg tablet
Half tablet twice a day with meal CONTD…

Enalapril 10 mg
Half tablet twice a day after meal CONTD…

Rkleen
One spoon once a day per orally CONTD
Report Advice:
Vet pro renal diet is strongly advised.
Please give all the medications exactly as advised. Ensure adequate water intake. Appetite may improve gradually after treatment. Regular follow up and repeat kidney tests are important to monitor progress.
Report Note:
Your pet is suffering from a kidney related problem due to which toxins and excess minerals can build up in the body and appetite may reduce.
Aluminum hydroxide has been prescribed to bind excess phosphorus in the gut which helps reduce further damage to kidneys.
Enalapril is given to protect kidney function and control blood pressure helping slow progression of kidney disease. Rkleen is prescribed to support kidney health and help remove waste products from the body. A prescription renal diet is advised as it is low in protein, phosphorous and salt which reduces the workload on the kidneys and helps maintain long term kidney health.

Report Note:
Hematology findings are consistent with an active systemic inflammatory response. The progressive lingual necrosis suggests significant vascular compromise, most likely secondary to inflammatory or septic vasculitis with associated microvascular thrombosis and tissue ischemia.

Peripheral smear demonstrates platelet clumping. In the present clinical setting, this is interpreted as in vivo platelet activation rather than an artefactual change. Ongoing endothelial injury and cytokine mediated platelet activation may be contributing to platelet aggregation and localized thrombus formation, further impairing perfusion to affected tissues. 

Underlying neoplasia remains a differential diagnosis pending histopathological evaluation. Definitive diagnosis will depend on biopsy findings.

Report Note:
Blood tests show elevated kidney values (SDMA and creatinine) indicating decreased kidney function. This is likely the cause of the current vomiting and reduced appetite. Supportive treatment including intravenous fluids and medications has been recommended to stabilize the patient and support kidney function if anorexia persists. If appetite is adequate, a renal diet is advised. A home-prepared renal friendly diet may also be given.

Suggested Home Diet for  Renal Support short 
	•	Boiled white rice
	•	Boiled skinless chicken (small quantity, well-cooked & no salt)
	•	Boiled egg white (avoid yolk)
	•	Boiled pumpkin 
All food should be bland, low salt and given in small frequent meals. Avoid high protein meats, organ meat, salty foods and treats. Further diet adjustment may be advised based on follow up results.

Diet Plan – 2 Month Old Pomeranian Puppy

Feed four small meals a day.

Morning (around 7 AM)
Soft boiled rice mixed with a small amount of finely shredded boiled chicken (no salt or spices).

Late Morning (around 11 AM)
Rice with boiled egg white. Add a few drops of omega supplement.

Afternoon (around 3 PM)
Rice with boiled chicken and a little boiled pumpkin or carrot (well mashed).

Evening (around 7 PM)
Same as morning meal or softened puppy starter food. Guwell powder can be mixed.

Food should be freshly prepared, soft and served in small portions. Clean drinking water should always be available.

Important:
Feed regularly (4 times) daily & keep the puppy warm and introduce any new food gradually.

Avoid:
Milk, salt, bones, organ meat, spicy/oily food and packaged human snacks.

Report History: Normal appetite

Report Reason: Another cat belonging to the same owner presented with a history of vomiting. Considering the recent history of illness in a companion cat from the same household and the similarity of clinical sign , Feline Panleukopenia was suspected.
Report Treatment: Supportive and symptomatic treatment was started immediately including fluid therapy and anti-vomiting medication. The same treatment protocol used for the previously affected cat was continued for 4 days.
Report Note:
The clinical signs and history of illness in multiple cats within the same household were suggestive of a contagious viral disease, most consistent with Feline Panleukopenia. The cat responded well to supportive management.
The cat showed gradual improvement during the treatment period. Vomiting subsided, appetite returned and the cat recovered clinically after 4 days of therapy.

MEDICATION
Gut Well
	•	Dose: ½ teaspoon orally, twice daily
	•	Duration: 5 days
	•	Purpose: To restore normal intestinal microflora and support gastrointestinal recovery.

PREVENTIVE HEALTH PLAN
Deworming:
	•	Date: 17 March
	•	Administer appropriate broad-spectrum anthelmintic as per body weight.
Vaccination:
	•	Date: 19 March
	•	Core vaccination recommended: Tricat Vaccine (for protection against Feline Panleukopenia, Feline Herpesvirus Infection and Feline Calicivirus Infection).

CLINICAL ADVICE
	•	Ensure adequate hydration and normal feeding.
	•	Monitor for vomiting, diarrhea or lethargy.
	•	Vaccination should only be administered if the cats remain clinically healthy on the scheduled date.

Follow-up was conducted and the medication was continued once daily, as Zico was in the recovery phase; however, vomiting had not been completely controlled at that time.

Additionally, gut care medication was provided. The owner was advised to mix 1 teaspoon of the medication in 10 ml of water and administer it orally with a syringe twice daily.

Report Advice:
Strict rest: Keep the cat confined (cage rest) to limit movement and prevent displacement of the fracture.
Bandage care: Keep the bandage clean and dry at all times. Do not allow it to get wet.
Daily monitoring: Check for swelling above or below the bandage, foul odor, discharge, cold toes or excessive licking/chewing.
Bandage integrity: If the bandage becomes loose, tight, wet or damaged, return to the clinic immediately.
Prevent self-trauma: Use an Elizabethan collar if the cat attempts to remove or chew the bandage.
Follow-up: Recheck and bandage change as advised (follow up after 7 days of bandage placement)
Appetite & activity: Report reduced appetite, lethargy or worsening lameness promptly.
Immediate veterinary attention is required if any signs of circulatory compromise or bandage related complications are observed.

Report Prescription: Cadistin inj & Meloxicam inj

Report Note:
Sample received 7th May 2026
Gross Description
Received a soft to firm nodular tissue mass from the mammary gland region. Cut surface appeared pale white to tan and lobulated.

Microscopic Findings
Sections examined show a well-circumscribed benign epithelial neoplasm composed of proliferative glandular structures lined by uniform cuboidal to columnar epithelial cells. The cells are arranged in acinar and tubular patterns supported by moderate fibrovascular stroma.

Cells exhibit minimal anisocytosis and anisokaryosis with low mitotic activity. No evidence of vascular invasion, necrosis or marked cellular atypia is observed. Surrounding connective tissue shows mild fibroplasia.

Impression
Mammary Adenoma (Benign Mammary Gland Tumor)

Comments
The histopathological features are consistent with a benign mammary adenoma. Complete surgical excision is generally curative. Regular monitoring is recommended for recurrence or development of additional mammary masses.

Gross Findings
Received multiple irregular soft tissue masses from mammary gland tissue. Cut surface appears grayish white to tan with focal cystic and firm areas.

Microscopic Findings
Sections examined reveal proliferation of neoplastic epithelial cells arranged in tubular, acinar and papillary patterns supported by moderate fibrous stroma. The neoplastic cells are pleomorphic with round to oval hyperchromatic nuclei and moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm. Anisocytosis and anisokaryosis are moderate to marked.
Mitotic figures are observed occasionally. Multifocal areas of necrosis and inflammatory cell infiltration are present. Invasion into surrounding connective tissue is evident.
Features are consistent with a malignant epithelial mammary neoplasm.

Histopathological Diagnosis
Mammary Gland Adenocarcinoma

Remarks
The lesion is malignant in nature with invasive characteristics. Multiple gland involvement may indicate multicentric mammary tumor development. Correlation with ovarian pathology is advised, particularly to evaluate possible hormonally active ovarian neoplasia.

Thoracic radiography and regional lymph node evaluation are recommended to assess metastatic spread.

* pH: 6.5

* Blood/Hemoglobin: Positive (++)

Microscopic Examination
* Red Blood Cells (RBCs): High per HPF
* White Blood Cells (WBCs): Occasional
* Epithelial Cells: Few
* Crystals: Not observed
* Bacteria: Not observed
* Casts: Not observed

Interpretation
The urine sample contains a significant number of red blood cells, indicating hematuria (blood in urine). No evidence of bacterial infection or urinary crystals was observed in the examined sample. According to the clinical history, the cat’s condition improved without medication, suggesting a possible self limiting episode of lower urinary tract irritation or inflammation.

Comment
Although clinical signs have resolved, recurrence of hematuria may occur in feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) and related urinary conditions. Monitoring of urination habits, water intake and recurrence of blood in urine is advised. Further diagnostic evaluation, including repeat urinalysis, urine culture, radiography or ultrasonography, may be recommended if signs recur.
